content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, print its minimum depth.
The minimum depth is the number of nodes along the shortest path from the root node down to the nearest leaf node.

For example, the minimum depth of the following binary tree is: 2




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line - the minimum depth of the given binary tree.
constraints: All pi and ci are distinct and form a single rooted binary tree
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next N-1 lines each contain three space-separated values: pi, ci, Ri, describing the tree with edge information - pi is an integer denoting the parent node of the ith edge, ci is the child node, and the letter Ri denotes whether the child is a left child or a right child, it's value is 'L' if ci is the left child of pi, else 'R'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a single line - the minimum depth of the given binary tree.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree has root 1. Its left child 2 is a leaf, so the shortest root-to-leaf path has length 2.
Both children of the root are leaves; the minimum depth is therefore 2.
Node 3 is a leaf directly under the root, giving a minimum depth of 2.

solution.approach: To find the minimum depth of a binary tree, we can use a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm. BFS is suitable for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph, which is equivalent to finding the minimum depth in a tree. We start from the root node and explore all its neighbors before moving on to the next level. This ensures that when we reach a leaf node, it is the one with the minimum depth.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the tree. The total time complexity is O(T * N) because in the worst case, we might have to traverse all nodes in the tree for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the nodes in the queue during BFS. The space complexity is linear with respect to the number of nodes in the tree because in the worst case, the queue will contain all nodes at the last level of the tree.
